summary:
Jenne-Jeno (c. 300 BC onward) in the inland Niger Delta of modern Mali was one of the earliest known urban settlements in sub-Saharan Africa, predating Islamic influence by over a millennium and demonstrating that urban civilization developed independently in West Africa without external stimulus — a finding that transformed scholarly understanding of African history.

Jenne-Jeno (Old Jenne) was excavated by archaeologists Roderick and Susan McIntosh in the 1970s–80s, revealing a complex settled community dating to around 250 BC that grew to cover over 33 hectares by its peak (around 800 AD). The site shows evidence of specialized craft production, long-distance trade (iron tools, copper from the Sahara, stone not locally available), and dense urban occupation without the monumental palaces or temples typical of other early urban sites — suggesting a form of urban heterarchy rather than top-down hierarchy. Its location in the inland Niger Delta gave access to fish, flood-recession agriculture, and trade routes. Jenne-Jeno demonstrates that West African urban development began independently and early, and that the later trans-Saharan trade empires (Ghana, Mali, Songhai) had deep indigenous roots.
